abstract: Metformin is a widely used first-line treatment for type 2 diabetes mellitus. It primarily acts by reducing hepatic glucose production and improving peripheral insulin sensitivity. Clinical trials have demonstrated that metformin significantly lowers HbA1c levels and reduces the risk of diabetes-related complications. Long-term use is generally well-tolerated, though gastrointestinal side effects such as nausea and diarrhea are common at initiation.

Aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid) has been associated with an increased risk of gastrointestinal bleeding in elderly patients. A retrospective cohort study of 12,000 patients found that low-dose aspirin therapy was associated with a 2.4-fold increased incidence of peptic ulcer disease compared to non-users. The risk was further elevated in patients with Helicobacter pylori infection. Co-administration of proton pump inhibitors was found to attenuate this adverse effect.

Doxorubicin, an anthracycline chemotherapeutic agent, is effective in treating breast cancer but is associated with dose-dependent cardiotoxicity leading to dilated cardiomyopathy. This study evaluated the cardioprotective effects of dexrazoxane in patients receiving doxorubicin-based chemotherapy for early-stage breast cancer. Patients receiving dexrazoxane showed significantly lower rates of cardiac dysfunction at 12-month follow-up. These findings support the use of dexrazoxane as a cardioprotective strategy during doxorubicin treatment.
